coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional red-emission graphene quantum dots with tunable light emissions for trace water sensing, WLEDs and information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yongsheng
Tang, Siyuan
Chen, Da
Wang, Changxing
Gu, Bingli
Li, Xiameng
Xie, Feng
Wang, Gang
Guo, Qinglei
coredata.dc:description: In this study, red-emission graphene quantum dots (R-GQDs), with tunable emission wavelength, are synthesized and explored for applications in trace water sensing, white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) and information encryption. Systematic experiments and characterizations demonstrate that the as-fabricated R-GQDs possess homogeneous size, good crystallinity and high doping concentration of nitrogen and oxygen. Moreover, tunable multicolor light emissions of R-GQDs are demonstrated by simply adjusting the polarity of surrounding solvents. As a result, solid-state fluorescence films are successfully fabricated to construct a high-quality WLED, with the CIE (Commission International d′Eclairage) coordinates (0.352, 0.380) and color temperature of 5121 K. Besides, the water quenchable fluorescence of R-GQDs enables the fabrication of a trace water sensing platform, with the low detection limit of 0.034%. Finally, the potential of our prepared R-GQDs for the application in color visual information encryption is developed. This study may pave the way for the development of GQDs, especially in optically sensing, optoelectronics and anti-counterfeiting technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: R-GQDs
Solvatochromic
Trace water sensing
WLEDs
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of trace water in organic solvents
Fabrication of WLEDs based on R-GQDs polymer films
Responses of R-GQDs to solvents for tunable light emission

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for synthesizing R-GQDs. (b) TEM image and Gaussian particle-size distribution of as-obtained R-GQDs. (c) HRTEM and (d) FFT image of R-GQDs. (e) AFM image of R-GQDs after transferred on a SiO2/Si substrate. (f) The statistic histogram of the height distribution of R-GQDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrums of C 1s (g), N 1s (h) and O 1s (i).

(a) PL, PLE and UV–vis absorption spectra of R-GQDs. Optical images are R-GQDs aqueous solution illuminated by sunlight (left) and a 365 nm UV light (right). (b) CIE chromaticity coordinate of the PL emission of R-GQDs. (c) PL spectra of R-GQDs recorded under different excitations from 420 nm to 520 nm. (d) The two-dimensional mapping of emission (λem) and excitation (λex) of R-GQDs. The fluorescence intensity of R-GQDs under (e) ultraviolet light irradiation (150 W and 365 nm mercury lamp), and (f) visible light irradiation for different timescales.

(a) Normalized PL spectra and (b) the UV–vis absorption spectra of the R-GQDs in 7 different solvents. The emission spectra of R-GQDs excited with different wavelengths in different solvents: toluene (c), ethyl acetate (d), acetone (e), ethanol (f), DMF (g), formamide (h), water (i).
Schematic illustration of the possible energy level diagram and multicolor emission processes of R-GQDs in various solvents.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-GQDs with different water contents in ethanol solvent. (b) The relationship between F/F0 and the water content in ethanol solvent. (c) Images of R-GQDs in the presence of various water contents under the UV light irradiation. (d) Emitting light spectrum of the as-prepared WLEDs. (e) CIE color coordinates of WLEDs. (f) Optical image of the light WLEDs. (g) Images of “Ningbo University” logo spayed by different solvents under the UV light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional red-emission graphene quantum dots with tunable light emissions for trace water sensing, WLEDs and information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, red-emission graphene quantum dots (R-GQDs), with tunable emission wavelength, are synthesized and explored for applications in trace water sensing, white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) and information encryption. Systematic experiments and characterizations demonstrate that the as-fabricated R-GQDs possess homogeneous size, good crystallinity and high doping concentration of nitrogen and oxygen. Moreover, tunable multicolor light emissions of R-GQDs are demonstrated by simply adjusting the polarity of surrounding solvents. As a result, solid-state fluorescence films are successfully fabricated to construct a high-quality WLED, with the CIE (Commission International d′Eclairage) coordinates (0.352, 0.380) and color temperature of 5121 K. Besides, the water quenchable fluorescence of R-GQDs enables the fabrication of a trace water sensing platform, with the low detection limit of 0.034%. Finally, the potential of our prepared R-GQDs for the application in color visual information encryption is developed. This study may pave the way for the development of GQDs, especially in optically sensing, optoelectronics and anti-counterfeiting technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Water, known as one of the most common substance in our life, is the most familiar impurity in the field of food processing, organic synthesis and environmental monitoring [1–3]. The presence of trace water could cause the hydrolysis of chemical reagent, thus boosting the formation of oxidation products [4,5]. Therefore, sensitive detection and accurate determination of trace water are extremely necessary. As a well-known method for such detection of water, Karl-Fischer titration has been widely employed since 1930s [6]. However, the usage of venomous chemicals and complex instruments for the Karl-Fischer titration, non-capable of real-time monitoring is inevitable during the practical applications [7,8]. Currently, luminescence-based fluorescent sensors have been introduced to replace of the conventional Karl-Fischer titration owing to their simple manipulations, high sensitivity, nontoxicity and ultrafast reaction time [9–11]. Plentiful materials, like metal-organic framework [12,13], luminescent copper nanoclusters [14,15], and polymers [16,17], have been extensively utilized as fluorescence sensing materials. However, the complicated synthesis processes, narrow linear detection range and inferior biocompatibility limit their wide-spread applications [6]. Therefore, it is highly demanded to develop a luminescent material to monitor trace water with a simple synthesis strategy, good biocompatibility and high-performance detection.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have attracted many concerns in recent years. Compared to organic fluorescence dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs have numerous advantages, such as excellent photoluminescence [18], adjustable wavelength [19], unique biocompatibility [20], easy-to-modify surface structures [21] and plentiful functional groups [22]. As a result, GQDs are considered as the promising candidate in bioimaging [23], fluorescence sensing [24], luminescent device [25], catalysis and supercapacitors [26]. In terms of fluorescence sensing in life metabolism and environmental monitor, GQDs also play significant roles due to their high-speed response, low detection limit and favorable biocompatibility. Some GQDs-based sensors have been exploited for the quantitative evaluation of water [6,27,28]. However, the light emission of current GQDs for water detection is limited within the short wavelength, such as blue, green or yellow, which is hard to apply in the field of photothermal therapy, optoelectronic devices, fluorescent sensing and bioimaging due to the strong photodamage of biology and short penetration in tissue [29]. Although the red emission GQDs, with a large emission wavelength, as the sensing platform have been reported [30,31], the corresponding quantum yield (QY), as an important factor to evaluate the property of GQDs, is extremely low (less than 6%).

Herein, red-emission graphene quantum dots (R-GQDs) with a high QY are successfully synthesized through utilizing p-phenylenediamine and melamine as precursors under the facile and gentle hydrothermal treatment. The as-synthesized R-GQDs show uniform size distribution and excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior with well-behaved stability. Notably, tunable light emissions of R-GQDs with the wavelength varying from 532 nm to 624 nm (from green to red) are realized by dispersing R-GQDs in different polar solutions. The QY reaches 29~35% in common organic solvents (such as toluene, ethyl acetate, acetone, ethanol, dimethyl formamide-DMF and formamide), whereas the photoluminescence (PL) intensity reduces linearly with the addition of water in a content range from 0% to 100%, revealing an ultralow detection limit of 0.034%. Moreover, the synthesized GQDs with solvatochromic properties are utilized to fabricate high-performances WLEDs, with the CIE coordinates of (0.352, 0.380), which are further explored for the application in visual information encryption. This work offers a prospective and novel thread for preparing the polychrome GQDs, the demonstrated examples of our prepared GQDs pave the way for the development of sensing, optoelectronic devices and anti-counterfeiting.

P-Phenylenediamine, melamine and other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were analytical grade (AR, 97%) and were used as received without further purification. Water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ was depurated via the Millipore system.

The morphology of R-GQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on Hitachi H-8100. Atomic force microscope (AFM, Oxford Instruments, Cypher S) was utilized to determine the size and height of the R-GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Ulvac-PHI) and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR, Bruker Ten-sor27) were employed to determine the chemical composition and functional groups. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/Max, Rigaku, Tokyo) was used to survey to structure of the R-GQDs. Raman spectra was analyzed by using an Ar+ laser with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The fluorescence spectra (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) and ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra (UV-5800 spectrophotometer) are introduced to investigate the optical properties of R-GQDs.

0.12 g melamine and 0.11 g p-Phenylenediamine were dissolved into 20 ml absolute ethyl alcohol with lightly stirring, then the mixture was transferred into a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated for 8 h at 200 °C. The as-prepared products were filtered against a 0.22 µm microporous membrane after cooling down to the room temperature, and dialyzed with ultra clean water through a dialysis membrane (100–500 Da MWCO) for 3 days. Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried and stored in a brown reagent bottle for future use.

Rhodamine 6 G in ethanol absolute (φ = 0.54) was chosen as the standard. The quantum yields of R-GQDs were calculated according to the following Equation [32],
(1)
φ =
φ
R
×
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2
Where φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, A represents the optical density, and the subscript R refers to the standard sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of trace water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: First, different concentrations (0–100 vol%) of water were prepared in ethanol, acetone, DMF and formamide. After that, R-GQDs powder was added at a weight concentration of 1 mg per liter. Then, the emission spectra were recorded by the PL spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 493 nm at the room temperature. For dependability, the final fluorescence intensity was the average of three times of independent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of WLEDs based on R-GQDs polymer films
ce:section.ce:para: Because the wavelength of the prepared R-GQDs can be adjusted from green to red by adjusting the solvent polarity, polystyrene (PS) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) were chosen to construct the green and red luminous film. Then according to the principle of three primary colors, the high-quality WLEDs were fabricated by covering the red and green luminous films on the blue LED chips (465 nm).

Fig. 1(a) schematically shows the synthesis process of R-GQDs, where melamine and p-Phenylenediamine are used as the precursors in ethyl alcohol solution. After the thermal annealing at 200 °C for 8 h, the synthesized R-GQDs exhibit nearly spherical shape and uniform size distribution with average diameter of 3.9 ± 0.6 nm (based on more than 50 dots), as shown in Fig. 1(b) with TEM results. The high resolution TEM image shown in Fig. 1(c) reveals a lattice spacing distance of 0.24 nm, corresponding to the representative (100) lattice fringe of graphene [33]. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis pattern appears in Fig. 1(d), and the hexagonal symmetry pattern can be obviously observed, suggesting a high crystalline structure of the R-GQDs [34]. The XRD analysis of the R-GQDs (see Fig. S1) exhibits a wide dispersed diffraction peak centered at 23.8°, which is attributed to the plane (002) of graphitic carbon [35]. Fig. 1(e) shows the AFM result of R-GQDs, revealing that the thicknesses are in the range of 0.7–1.4 nm. Statistics of the height distribution in the AFM image demonstrates the average height is 0.9 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(f), indicating that the synthesized R-GQDs have 2–3 layers of graphene [36]. The high-degree graphitization is established by Raman spectrum in Fig. S2, where the signals around 1357 and 1580 cm−1 are assigned to the disordered D band and G band. The ratio of ID/IG is calculated as 0.457, manifestly evidencing the high graphitization [37].

The element composition of as-prepared R-GQDs is investigated by XPS. As highlighted in Fig. S3, three characteristic signals of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s at 285, 398 and 532 eV are found, and the atomic percentages are 37.91%, 26.92% and 35.17%, respectively. The existence of the oxygen element can be attributed to the ethanol involved in the carbonization reaction. Noteworthy, the R-GQDs have much higher doping percentages of N and O compared to present reports [38–40]. High nitrogen and oxygen content can change the surface states of R-GQDs, resulting in a lower energy level that leads to the long wavelength emission [41]. The high-resolution C 1s spectra in Fig. 1(g) could be deconvoluted into four peaks locating at 284.1, 285.2, 286.8 and 288 eV, which represent C‒C/C = C, C = N, C–N and C‒O, respectively [42,43]. Three peaks representing pyridinic N (398.9 eV), Graphite N (399.8 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.9 eV) are also found in the N 1s spectrum [44], as shown in Fig. 1(h). For the O spectrum, two peaks at 531.8 and 532.9 eV, which are assigned as the C˭O/O‒H and C‒O bonds [45], are displayed in Fig. 1(i). Furthermore, the surface functional groups of R-GQDs are characterized by FTIR. As disclosed in Fig. S4, the broad vibration peaks within 3200–3600 cm−1 are attributed to -OH and -NH2 groups. The bending vibrations of C ˭ O/C ˭ N and C ˭ C are situated at 1629 and 1516 cm−1 [46], while the stretching vibrations of C‒N and C‒O‒C appear at 1246 cm−1 and 1140 cm−1 [47]. The above XPS and FTIR results indicate that N atoms have been successfully doped in R-GQDs, and the surface of the R-GQDs have been functionalized by a large number of nitrogen or oxygen related chemical groups.

The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption are powerful tools to survey the optical properties of R-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-GQDs shows a broad absorption, almost covering the entire 400–600 nm visible band. The absorption band at 240 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of graphite sp2 [37], and the peaks at 310 and 500 nm result from n-π * transition of C = O/C ˭ N [48]. Moreover, there is an overlap between the absorbance and PL excitation spectrum, indicating that the nitrogen- and oxygen-related surface states contribute to the intense red emission [46]. R-GQDs dispersed in water is brown under sunlight, but presents bright red fluorescence under the UV irradiation (365 nm). The PL emission peak is 600 nm and the PL excitation peak at 493 nm. Moreover, the as-prepared R-GQDs exhibit extremely high QY (about 30%) [44,49], which is obtained by utilizing an integrated sphere under the excitation of 360 nm. The CIE coordinate of the maximum PL spectrum is (0.582, 0.413), as shown in Fig. 2(b). As shown in Fig. 2(c), the emission wavelength of the as-synthesized R-GQDs exhibit an excitation-independent behavior under various excitations with different wavelengths, which is attributed to the homogeneous surface states [50]. Fig. 2(d) reveals a more intuitive view of the relationship between emission and excitation of R-GQDs by the two-dimensional mapping of emission (λem) and excitation (λex), and a small and nearly circular area (at λex = 493 nm, λem = 600 nm) is displayed.

For the red emission mechanism of GQDs, two popular models, i.e., the sp2-conjugated size and surface states control emission, are adopted [51]. In this work, however, the effective red emission of R-GQDs is heavily attributed to the abundant oxygen/nitrogen-related surface groups. Firstly, based on FTIR and UV–vis results, the R-GQDs are demonstrated to have a large number of nitrogen and oxygen functional groups and nitrogen-derived structures, capable of contributing to the red emission [46]. More importantly, Raman and XPS analyses show the R-GQDs are highly graphitized with a large percentage of graphitic nitrogen. Therefore, extra electrons will exist in the conjugated system and inpour into the unoccupied π * orbital. As a result, the bandgap of R-GQDs will be efficaciously reduced, thus leading to the long wavelength emission [44,52]. Results in Fig. S5 obtained by the acid-base treatment can further confirm above analyses. In alkaline conditions, the deprotonation degree of nitrogen derivatives increases with the increase of pH value, and the transfer of protons from the nitrogen derivatives to conjugated carbon structure occurs, thus decreasing the fluorescence intensity of R-GQDs. Under acidic conditions, however, the protonation process involving the destruction of nitrogen and oxygen species gradually increases as the pH value decreases from 7 to 3, thus resulting in the decrease of the luminescent center [53]. The distinct pH dependent emission of R-GQDs further proves that nitrogenous and oxygenated surface states are the source of the emission center.

For practical applications in fluorescence-based optoelectronic devices or probes, the emission stabilities of R-GQDs under different conditions are required. As shown in Fig. 2(e), the fluorescence intensity keeps 92.9% after the ultraviolet exposure (365 nm) for 14 h. Only the 10.5% decrease is found after storing at the room temperature for 14 days, as demonstrated in Fig. 2(f). These results indicate that the composited R-GQDs possess excellent anti-photobleaching. Fig. S6 shows the thermostability test of the R-GQDs. The fluorescence intensity almost has no noticeable change until the heating temperature over 100 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses of R-GQDs to solvents for tunable light emission
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the optical properties of the R-GQDs can be affected by the surroundings, the PL of the as-prepared R-GQDs dissolved in different solvents, including toluene, ethyl acetate, acetone, ethanol, DMF, formamide and water are studied. Fig. 3(a) depicts the normalized PL emission spectra of R-GQDs distributed in 7 different solvents, and an obvious red-shift phenomenon with the maximum PL emission peaks locating at 532, 549, 562, 600, 574, 604 and 624 nm in toluene, ethyl acetate, acetone, ethanol, DMF, formamide and water solvents, respectively, is revealed. The corresponding emission colors and the maximum emission wavelengths can be observed in Fig. S7(a–b). The emission wavelength gradually red shifts with the increase of the solvent polarity. However, the ethanol solution shows a bit abnormal, which is ascribed to the formation of hydrogen bonding between ethanol and R-GQDs. According to the above results, the multicolor emission GQDs solutions can be precisely obtained via simply tuning the surrounding solvents of R-GQDs.

To explain the solvent-dependent characteristics of R-GQDs, UV-Vis absorption properties are characterized. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the increase of the solvent polarity induces a red-shift of the absorption wavelength, indicating that the R-GQDs possess different energy gaps in different solvents. The bandgaps are calculated in Fig. S7(c–i). As the polarity increases, the energy gap gradually declines (expect for ethanol), which is accordance with the red shift of PL emission. Previous investigations demonstrated that the solvent-dependent PL was caused by various emissive defects on the surface of GQDs [54]. As shown in Fig. 3(c–i), the emission excitation wavelength of R-GQDs is irrelevant to the excitation wavelength in different solvents, further confirming that the shrinks of energy gap are generated from the exposure of surface states to various solvents [55]. The representative two-dimensional contour mappings of emission and excitation in Fig. S8 gives more details to reflect the red-shift of the emission center with the increased polarity. Meanwhile, images of R-GQDs dispersed in different solvents are captured in Fig. S9 under different excitations. As the excitation wavelength progressively increases, the fluorescence intensity first increases and then gradually decreases. To further explore the mechanism of solvent-dependent emissions, especially for the abnormal emission in ethanol, R-GQDs are dispersed in different monohydric alcohol solutions, including methanol, ethanol, 1-propanol, 1-butanol, 1-pentanol. Although the polarity decreases sequentially from methanol to 1-pentanol, the PL emission and UV-Vis absorption spectra have not obvious difference, as shown in Fig. S10, suggesting the fluorescence states of R-GQDs in these alcohol solvents change punily with the variations of polarity. In alcohol and water solvents, the -OH, -COOH and -NH2 groups are easy to form strong hydrogen bonds with the oxygen/nitrogen groups of R-GQDs, thus triggering this phenomenon [56].

In view of the above results, a visual illustration of possible energy level diagram and emission processes of the R-GQDs in various solvents is exhibited in Fig. 4. From the XPS and FTIR results, the synthesized R-GQDs should possess abundant functional groups, like -OH, -NH2, -COOH. In aprotic solvents (such as toluene and ethyl acetate), referring to the previous reports [57,58], strong interactions between the dipole moment of solvent molecules and R-GQDs occur, thus significantly influencing on the surface electronic states. With the increase of solvent polarity, the excited and ground state charges can be stabilized by transferring electrons from functional groups to the solvent molecules, thus leading to a redshift of the emission spectrum. In protic solvents (such as ethanol and water), the emission wavelength also exhibits a red-shift trend, which can be attributed to the intense hydrogen bonds between solvent molecules and R-GQDs surface states. Therefore, the charge carrier density are effectively enhanced, resulting in a gradual decrease of the emission energy level [45]. However, in acetone, DMF, formamide solvents, the combination of the weak hydrogen bond interactions and the dipole-dipole interactions in solvents generates new stable energy levels, which also allows for the excitation-independent long-wavelength emission.

Inspired by the unique optical performances and good solubility in organic solutions, a simple, sensitive and efficient fluorescence-based sensing system is developed to monitor the trace water in organic solvents. As shown in Fig. S11, when 20% v/v water is added to the R-GQDs solution in ethanol, the PL intensity is acutely decreased within one minute and then keeps stabilized, suggesting the capability of detecting trace water. Fig. 5(a) reveals the fluorescence spectra of R-GQDs dispersed in ethanol solution containing different amounts of water (from 0% to 100%). As the content of water increases, the PL intensity gradually decreases with a slight red-shift of the peak position. The addition of water facilitates to form a strong hydrogen-bonded network between the R-GQDs and water molecules, which increases non-radiative relaxation pathways by enhanced vibronic coupling, leading to an intense quenching of the PL emission [59,60]. The red-shift phenomenon is caused by the increased polarity of the solution due to the addition of water. The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) and the content of water is given in Fig. 5(b), where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of R-GQDs with and without water in organic solvents. Notably, there are two different linear intervals. When the water content is less than 20%, the change of F/F0 is more obvious than that of the larger water content (> 20%). Setting the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) equal to 3 as the standard, the detection limit is calculated as 0.034%. Fig. 5(c) visually shows the optical images of R-GQDs with different water contents under the 365 nm UV light irradiation. The fluorescence intensity is severely decline with the addition of water, and there is almost no fluorescence in the pure water. Besides ethanol solvent, we also employ R-GQDs to detection water in acetone, DMF, and formamide. As depicted in Fig. S12, similar results as ethanol are found. The detection limit in acetone, DMF, and formamide solvents is calculated to be 0.075%, 0.111% and 0.065%, respectively. Table S1 summarizes current studies about utilizing GQDs-based fluorescence materials for detecting trace water, and the R-GQDs-based sensing platform in this work has a much higher sensitivity and wider detection range, suggesting great potentials in environmental monitoring.

The ability of tunable light emissions by surrounding solvents represents significant potentials of R-GQDs for the fabrication of WLEDs. Based on the principle of three primary colors, we attempt to construct solid light-emitting films with different emission colors. PS and PEG are chosen to construct green and red fluorescence films because the inner groups are resemble to the corresponding solvents [50]. For the preparation of luminous films, the polymers are dissolved in organic solvents containing R-GQDs to from the uniform solution. After that, the aqueous polymer/R-GQDs/solvent is transferred into a clean mold, then dried at room temperature. As shown in Fig. S13, the PL emission peaks of prepared luminous films are located at 552 nm and 602 nm. The inset shows strong fluorescence image under the ultraviolet light, implying the successful transfer of the polarity-dependent trait to solid-state luminescence. Finally, WLEDs are assembled by covering the green and red fluorescence films on a blue LED chip (465 nm). As shown in Fig. 5(d), the fluorescence spectrum of the as-constructed WLEDs covers the entire visible light range. The CIE coordinates of the as-fabricated WLEDs is calculated as (0.352, 0.380) with a color temperature of 5121 K. The color point is laid on the black body Planckian locus in Fig. 5(e). In addition, the WLEDs express a high color rendering index (about 83.5), and the luminous efficacy reaches 52.18 lm/w. The as-fabricated WLED, turned on at 3.0 V, has a high-quality white light, as exhibited in Fig. 5(f), and the color of “Ningbo University” logo can be legibly and veritably displayed.

Fig. 5(g) demonstrates the application of solvent-dependent R-GQDs in the field of anti-counterfeiting. The R-GQDs-based ink for anti-counterfeiting is shown in Fig. S14, and the pattern of “Ningbo University” is drawn on a rice paper. The drawn pattern emits red fluorescence under ultraviolet light. However, the fluorescence disappears after a while, which is caused by the quenching effect in solid/powder state because the ethanol has strong volatility. Most surprisingly, when we spray toluene, ethyl acetate, acetone, DMF and formamide solvents, respectively, the emission pattern reappears with obvious green, light yellow, dark yellow, orange and red colors, respectively. The simple operation process and satisfactory results indicate that our prepared R-GQDs have tremendous potentials in the field of information encryption.

In summary, multifunctional R-GQDs with tunable light emissions have been synthesized and explored for applications in trace water sensing, WLEDs and information encryption. The as-prepared R-GQDs exhibited obvious solvent-dependent light emissions that multicolor emission by R-GQDs solutions are obtained via simple adjustment of the surrounding solvents. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of R-GQDs is strongly depended on the water content. Therefore, a sensitive water-sensing probe in organic solvents, with the detection limit of 0.034%, is demonstrated. Moreover, solid-state luminescence films with different emission wavelengths is realized and utilized to fabricate WLEDs. Finally, the visual information encryption technology is proposed and demonstrated by our prepared R-GQDs. Our work may provide material strategies and device architectures for developing the applications of GQDs in fluorescence sensing, optoelectronic devices and anti-counterfeiting.

Yongsheng Yang: Analyzed experimental results and wrote the manuscript. Siyuan Tang: Assisted in analyzing experimental results. Da Chen: Conducted the experiments, Funding acquisition. Changxing Wang: Assisted in analyzing experimental results, Software. Bingli Gu: Assisted in analyzing experimental results, Software. Xiameng Li: Assisted in investigation, Formal analysis. Feng Xie: Assisted in investigation, Formal analysis. Gang Wang: Assisted in project administration. Qinglei Guo: Writing - review & editing. All authors read and contributed to the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084, and 51802337). K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104). The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.126593.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescence lifetime-based solid sensor for water
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Chang, Z. Murtaza, J. Lakowicz, G. Rao, A fluorescence lifetime-based solid sensor for water, Anal. Chim. Acta 350 (1997) 97‐104.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for the detection of humidity or water content
Chem. Soc. Rev.
